This could have been an excellent book. Emphasis on COULD HAVE.Had KR put more effort into story creation, world building and character development and less in sex scenes this would have been an amazing original book. As it is, this book was somewhat entertaining, mildly boring but mostly frustrating. If you’ve spent any time studying the Greek Classics or mythology you will hate this book. It was a completely inauthentic, unresearched retelling of the Persephone and Hades fable. The names of the characters and places were drawn from Greek myth but that’s pretty much where it ends. So throw away any prior knowledge you have of the old story if you want to get any enjoyment out of this. I’m not going to go into any detail of why this story didn’t follow the real one and give the author the benefit of the doubt, perhaps she intentionally went off book and didn’t intend to stick to the original.The real issue with this story was the lack of effort put into world building and plot development. A ton of crucial detail was omitted by Roberts leaving so many questions as to what was actually going on in the world she had built.For example:1. What and where exactly is Olympus? The characters have Greek names, have modern technology like cell phones and computers, and there are sky scrapers and cars and we are told that it’s a city, so I’m guessing it’s not the magical mountain where the mythical Zeus is said to have reigned. We do have a grounding moment at the beginning when Persephone mentions she wants to attend Berkeley in California for her PhD, so the real world does exist but we are never informed how Olympus fits into this world. It is mentioned that it takes a lot of effort and money to LEAVE Olympus, which has me thinking that there are magical elements at play (like why it’s difficult to cross the river Styx without an invitation from Hades) but nothing is ever clarified and everything is crazy vague. So Olympus is a city somewhere on planet earth maybe. But the people living there, including the characters named for gods, are human, as the book does tell us they are human a few times. I was entirely frustrated by the setting of the book. Giving us a few details would have grounded the place and allowed me to better immerse myself in the story.2. Who and what are the 13? So these all powerful rulers of Olympus, named for the Greek Gods, rule this city. Some by heritage, Zeus, Hades and Poseidon are titles handed down from father to son, and the remaining are appointed somehow with the details as to how never specified. So are they rulers? Are they criminals or mafia type people who control the city through nefarious means? Do they have magical powers? It seems that Hermes might? Again never specified. As this was a huge component of the story I really needed more information on who these people were and what exactly made them powerful. We kept getting told that if Hades broke the treaty they would all come down on him, but we are never told how. It didn’t seem like they had any means to harm him as he operated in a completely separate area of the city and had no real dependence on them. So all their threats seemed to lack any real power to me. Demeter cut off supplies to the lower city and it didn’t effect them one bit, so why do we even care that the 13 can come down on Hades? Clarification was badly needed here.3. Why was Hades forced to stay in the lower city and what exactly was the treaty? This treaty that made Hades unable to cross the Styx into the upper city was never clarified…we just keep hearing ‘don’t break the treaty’ blah blah but why and what is this treaty? No info is given.4. Why was Hades a myth? It seemed that everyone knew about him so his myth was the worst kept secret in Olympus. That whole part of the plot made little sense. It seemed that his existence was only really not known by Persephone and her sisters. And what was the point of it anyway? It didn’t really drive the plot except maybe offering some mystery to him at the beginning.There are more holes in the plot but I think the above are the major ones.So I was quite captivated at the beginning of the book because I felt that all this information was going to reveal itself over time, answering all these interesting questions for me. But as I got to the middle of the book I started to realize that I was going to get no further details at which point my interest in the story fell off significantly. It didn’t help that the middle of the book mainly consisted of many, many sex scenes between Hades and Persephone, some public, during sex parties meant to piss off Zeus, and the rest just the sex they were having on an hourly basis around his home. It got to be so that the sex was repetitive and mundane (yes public sex at sex parties was boring) so I just skimmed most of it.The character development in this book could have also been much better, I think this author just isn’t good at relaying detail. Whether it’s in her head and she has trouble getting it on page or its that she hasn’t even thought it out at all is unclear. Regardless, the characters and their motivation were one dimensional and, as a result, uninteresting.Demeter, Persephone’s mother, engages her to the wife murdering Zeus, and then acts as a pseudo villain in the book, but it’s never clarified as to why she did this and who she really is as a person. There was an opportunity to make her a very interesting villain with a complex backstory but that wasn’t done. She was basically just a pushy overly ambitious mother who’s only allegiance was to herself… she had the complexity of an early Bond villain.Persephone, the heroine, was never fully fleshed out. She was beautiful and sweet and horny. Other than that nothing is ever really developed in her. It says she wanted to pursue her PhD and leave the city but no details beyond that were given. PhD in what? What were her life goals? What was she going to do with herself when she ends up with Hades (not a spoiler, it’s a romance, they end up together). I guess she will just be his live in sex doll as the epilogue was just one long sex scene.Hades was probably the best developed thing in this book. His character had decent backstory, and he was fairly complex. I would have preferred if he wasn’t described as having long hair and a long beard, as I pictured Gandalf in my head, and would have preferred a more clean cut devil type character like Tom Ellis in Lucifer. But that’s preference so didn’t effect my rating.So this book was ok but greatly disappointing for me. The cover is amazing, the idea so intriguing and the start was killer but after about chapter five it lost momentum and just limped it’s way to the ending. Even the big vengeful finish was a cop out. It could have been so good but alas, the author didn’t have the imagination to take it there.

I really love Hades and Persephone the most but I don't know why none of their retellings ever fulfilled my expectation. And in all honestly, this book has an almost nonexistent plot with 85% smut scenes. Like, I love me some steamy romance book but this felt overdone that I get tired of reading them sometimes, ngl.However, owing to the wonderful embodiments of Hades and Persephone, I find myself enjoying this ♡ And I think your reason, feeling as well as circumstances when reading the book all play a part in considering whether you'll like it more or not. Because this book just quenches my romance thirst ass that was deprived after reading dreadful after dreadful books with lousy romance. Hence, the four stars.What I love the most about this book is definitely:Hades and Persephone. I love the excellent development in their romance. The banter between the two and how complimentary they are with one another. Like how excited Persephone was when Hades went and brought her to discover his places or just the moment they spent time together and Hades realized he is in love. That moment of realization!!! I AM IN LOVE WITH HADES IN LOVE!!!! Their relationship just strikes a mix between hot moments, kinky goodness, real love and compassion. And their chemistry?? Out of this world. I enjoy every bit of it (Just tone down a bit the horniness is all) but they're still so endearing.Also, this book offers me anew takewhere this is not really the magical Greek God retelling but mere ordinary people in the modern world. It still feels nice and interesting how Katee Robert tried to attribute what is often associated with these Godswho are not exactly a Godsuch when Hades went to the pet shop and named the three dogs he adopted as Cereberus. Or Demeter whose position is to provide the food supply in Olympus parallel to the real Goddess Demeter.Although Persephone's character probably did not truly enhance as the real Goddess Persephone, if we were to look in a figurative manner, I think Persephone here is still well represented as goddess of spring growth. Seeing how much she has supported Hades and let him grow from his past and earning his place in the Thirteen Houses.It's just still a bit bummer because there were no magical powers to inflate the characters' potential to make the book more captivating and engaging. And the plot is stillnot adequate to carry the whole novel, added the lack of world-building which was kind of a letdown. I know this supposed to be a book that is heavy with romance as its focus but if they could balance out all the conflicts, plot and world-building, I think this book would've been perfect. And maybe just the right Hades and Persephone I'm looking for.Still a good and sinful read, if you need one 😉

Her safe word is Pomegranate!The following ratings are out of 5:Narration: 🎧🎧Romance: 💋💋💋💋💋Heat/Steam: 🔥🔥🔥🔥🔥Story/Plot: 📙📕📗📘📔World building: 🌎🌏🌍🌎🌏Character development: 👨🏻👱🏻‍♀‍🧔🏻👩🏻The heroine:Persephone- Daughter to Demeter, one of the thirteen who rule the upper city. The Thirteen are the most powerful people in Olympus, they take on the names of the original Gods. Zeus is the most powerful. He is over 50 years old, cruel and has killed his previous 3 wives. Demeter and Zeus worked together to push Persephone into an engagement of Zeus. She couldn’t say no without angering both, but as soon as the engagement is announced she escapes the party and takes off.The Hero(es):Hades- He was one of the thirteen but many think he died. He rules the lower city on the other side of the river Styx. There is a treaty which says that anyone that makes it across one of the bridges over the river Styx belongs to Hades.The Story:While running from Zeus, Persephone finds that two of his minions are following her, and she makes it over one of the bridges to Hades. He captures her and brings him to his home.Let’s start with the narration. I absolutely hated the man’s voice. First it was a man with an British accent. I could be biased, because I am American, but British accents sound a bit too proper for the Lord of the Underworld. His voice isn’t deep enough either. If his voice was deep enough, I wouldn’t mind the accent but he sounded a bit foppish as is. Also his female voice was horrendous.The female narrator had a perfect voice for Persephone. She also had a British accent and sounded a bit like Felicity Jones. I liked her quite a lot, but her voice for Hades was also horrendous. I wish when they have dual narrators, they would have the males read all the male lines and female all the female lines no matter whose POV the chapter is in.The story was good, the romance was both romantic and super steamy. Though I did think it was odd that Hades was such a good guy and Zeus such a bad guy (though he wasn’t all that different in the myths). I was expecting Hades to be much darker than he was. Though I did like Hades as a person.The only real criticism I have with the story is the fact that it wasn’t really clear about the fact that when each of the thirteen die, someone eventually takes their place and name. For instance Persephone grew up on a farm until her mother became Demeter and with that she owned a high rise in the upper city of Olympus. So how do they get to be one of the thirteen and are they Gods in some way or just the richest & most powerful (which is what I thought), but then if that is true, how are they chosen?I did like how the author pieced in different parts of the myth into the story. Like the River Styx, the pomegranate (in the myth Persephone is not supposed to eat in the underworld and she eats six pomegranate seeds), the bargain with Demeter (where because Persephone ate six seeds she ends up spending six months with her mother and six with Hades every year), Charon (the ferryman to the underworld) and Cerberus (the three headed dog). I won’t tell how any of these things are incorporated into the story, but I loved that these and more were included.I voluntarily read & reviewed an advanced copy of this book. All thoughts & opinions are my own.Blog|Goodreads|Facebook|Instagram|Twitter|BookBub

I'm a simple gal. If you tell me there is a Hades and Persephone retelling written by Katee Robert available for request via NetGalley, I will run downstairs in my shower cap and towel to request it immediately.

This is complicated.The tl;dr is that, while I really loved elements of this, I didn’t like it as much as the first three Wicked Villain books.The not tl;dr is … it’s complicated.One of the things I admire about KR as an author is she’s super … mindful, I think, about what she’s doing. Or, y’know, most things that she’s doing. Nobody can be mindful about everything because then they’d be omniscient. But basically there are things she’s focused on—things she’s interested in and, I assume, thinks are important—and everything else can kind of go fuck itself.I can’t deny that I admire this as an approach to one’s own work.Although it does mean as a reader you can either get on the KR train or you can be smooshed by the KR train as it steams past. With Neon Gods, I sort of felt like I was running alongside the KR train, desperate to get on board, but somehow I didn’t quite make it? And I can’t quite figure out whether that’s because I was late at the station or because the, err, KR book train only opened its doors for, like, 3.5 seconds.I need to drop this metaphor.Basically, there’s zero point banging on about the world-building here. One does not read KR for the world-building. In a KR book, the plot is ONLY (and kind of explicitly) there to facilitate the emotional and sexual connection of the characters. And, y’know, that’s fine. That’s what I mean about getting on the KR train. However, I think this is where I first began struggling with Neon Gods: the world-building is flimsy and nonsensical (and that’s what I was prepared for) but it’s also … it also takes up a lot of page count. And I don’t mean in a “this reader is only here for the kinky sexing" sense, but it was a bit weird to find myself spending so much time wading through something that not only I wasn’t super interested in, it felt like the author wasn’t super interested in either.Although this might also be a “first book in a series” issue, because first books in a series have to bear the double-weight of their own story AND establishing the framework for a bunch of other stories too.Whinging aside, all of the things I really adore about KR’s work are present in Neon Gods. As ever, her approach to consent is fucking immaculate. Exhibitionism is sort of the kink du jour here (and it’s especially interesting because the heroine is more about it than the hero, but he’s into it because she is, which is not a dynamic I see explored very often) and the heroine gives her verbal consent to intense public sex, that—in the moment—neither of them decide they want to go through with on that particular occasion. I just really appreciate this sort of stuff: the reminder that consent is a spectrum and can (and should) be withdrawn at any time, and even the idea that you can consent to something fully meaningfully in abstract, but that doesn’t mean you also have to consent to the reality of it if you aren’t feeling it.That’s just … so hot to me. And feels like such a necessary pushback to the toxic and annoyingly persistent idea that consent isn’t or can’t be sexy.Similarly—and I’ve seen in this in Wicked Villains too—I really love how much flexibility KR builds into her sex scenes, and this carries through into Neon Gods. A lot of romances tend to a portray kink as kind of a … static thing? Like the dom/me says “Imma do this thing to you” and the sub is “ooh, yay” and then the dom does that thing, and then there’s aftercare. But in a KR book scenes shift, adapt and sometimes stop altogether in response to the in-the-moment needs of her characters.Again. That’s just … so hot to me.Plus, you’ve got the usual KR themes: active submission, heroines with agency, in this particular book the notion that allowing yourself to be cared for isn’t weakness, it’s a strength. All that stuff I am so very very into. Except I still didn’t quite connect to Neon Gods as much as I wanted to. I think due to the world-building issue I mentioned above, an unsatisfying conclusion (from both a plot and romantic perspective) and wanting—of all things—a bit more from Persephone. There’s lots to like about her, she’s strong, and determined, and not an idiot, and there’s a wonderful twist where the Hades/Persephone bargain is her initiative, not his (thus subverting the romance sex-bargain trope, which is usually insisted upon by the man, and therefore, frankly, more than a little problematic) but I never really felt I knew who she was.We’re told who she is, a fair bit. Hades keeps comparing her to sunshine, and her backstory is very much about pretending to be Demeter’s “perfect” daughter in the eyes of the press. To the extent that I was eventually imagining her as a Greek Blake Lively. But none of this felt narratively real and so it was hard to engage with her actual, y’know, arc? The way she changes—how she grows, finds herself and is empowered—through her choices and her connection with Hades.Hades, though. What an utter darling. I’ll take five.Seriously, though. He’s one of KR’s … I hesitate to use the word “soft” because of its negative connotations. How about … more human doms? Which is to say, he has more in common with Hook in A Worthy Opponent than Hades in Learn My Lesson—although he’s still very much his own person and, perhaps, one of KR’s more emotionally vulnerable heroes? Again, we’re told he’s grumpy and brooding and whatever, but he’s clearly a complete melt from the second he’s on page (right down to the fact that Hermes and Dionysus, who I very much enjoyed, are basically treating his house, and his fridge, like their own). There’s even puppies later. Actual puppies.Something I sincerely loved about the book was that both Hades and Persephone have reciprocal arcs around allowing themselves to *be* cared for and *be* protected. The scenes where Persephone demonstrates to Hades that she’s as alert to, and preoccupied with, his needs as he is with hers are absolutely feels-hitting. Plus there’s a “hero reveals his literal scars” scene that is tender and didn’t seem to be focused on milking the ol' manpain. So that was nice. And I did appreciate that Hades was allowed to seek, need and accept this kind of sweetness / care-taking from Persephone without it negatively impacting either his masculinity or his domliness. Again, this is something I’d like to see more of in general.Something that should not feel subversive but nevertheless always does.Anyway: I think, having babbled about this for ages now, I wish there’d been more space on page for Hades and Persephone to be who are they are, instead of pushing them towards a tropey grumpy/sunshine dynamic that, actually, served to diminish the strength of their connection and the nuances of their characterisation. Plus I wonder if the myth backdrop (and let me very clear, I am absolutely not getting into whether I feel this was a “fair” or “accurate” re-telling of a myth, it’s a bloody myth, it can be re-told however) was meant to act much like Disney movies do in Wicked Villains, only with less likelihood of severe and immediate legal action from a large and evil corporation. Because obviously when it comes to a character like Jasmine (for example) it’s the easiest thing in the world to make the imaginative leaps the author is expecting: I mean, OBVIOUSLY she has Daddy issues, OBVIOUSLY she feels trapped by her life (she literally has a song about it in Aladdin). I just don’t think myths work quite as effectively for providing that background: partly because they belong to a different cultural context and partly because there’s so many versions of them. I think, if I squint, I can get to the idea that Persephone as a mythical archetype could be seen as the daughter of an overbearing mother forced into a mould of public perfection. But it doesn’t feel as natural as or as obvious to me as, say, Meg is a damaged switch and Hercules is an adorable sub.The other thing that left me a bit quizzical was the role, or lack of role, queerness played in the book. Now, this is one is really difficult because I think it’s a given of any KR universe that all characters are bisexual. And, on the one hand, that’s great. We live in a default-heterosexual universe right now, and it, frankly, sucks. So, yes, I love the idea of a default-queer universe (to be honest, I think I tend to write default-queer universes myself) and I think it’s valuable to have a writer (to have writers) just occupy that kind of space without fanfare or explanation. It’s normalising and inclusive and positive. Yay. The problem is though (and this is a ‘complexity of representation’ problem, not a KR problem) that it can also feel … tokenistic. And I honestly don’t know how to navigate that.I think Neon Gods has been the most heteronormative-seeming book of KR’s that I’ve read, since Desperate Measures, when I was grumpy that Jafar wasn’t more queer-coded (though Jasmine is, of course, enthusiastically bisexual as fuck). Both Persephone and Hades are, we can assume, bisexual both by dint of living in a KR universe, but also because she references an ex-girlfriend, and he references an ex-boyfriend. However, they’re also pretty much each other-sexual for the entire duration of the book. And this is fine. This should be fine.After all, not like every couple needs to be having a multi-gender 5some every Tuesday evening to prove their queerness. And queer attraction is not … just one thing. A sexuality that is primarily partner-focused is neither queer nor straight, and it’s messy to insist that queer sexuality is always addressed through explicit on page attraction to multiple genders. I mean, if pansexual characters go around constantly lusting after everyone then you’re straying perilously close to the spiked pit of the “slutty pansexual” stereotype. On top of which, it’s perfectly possible—perfectly normal—to experience queer attraction without queerness feeling like much of your identity. And that too is fine. This should be fine.So the minimal bisexuality of Hades and Persephone should also be fine. And rationally I think it is. Not that I am the arbiter of fine for other people’s depiction of queerness. But it still felt … odd. And that is not me saying the book is bad, or the author has done a bad (that’s not my call to my make), or that bisexuality has to be portrayed a certain way, or that it only counts as bisexuality if both characters are genitals deep in every possible gender-identity available to them. But I think there’s a really narrow line between a character’s queerness being irrelevant in a way that feels positive and affirming, and a character’s queerness being irrelevant in a way that just feels … irrelevant.And I don’t know where that line is. And I think it’s probably different for different people.Plus, there’s a really odd bit where Hades is getting all romance-possessive on Persephone and he says:“Someday, when you let some asshole seduce you and you’re riding their cock, remember tonight and know that they will never compare to me. You think of me when they’re inside you.”Which just seems like a really …I’ll stick with odd … thing to say to a woman you know is bisexual when you are bisexual yourself. I mean, I appreciate that it’s a non-gendered cock she’s riding in this hypothetical scenario. But, like, surely if you’re that possessive you would care about ANY lover, not just a lover with a penis? Because otherwise it kind of seems like Hades is saying love/sex between people of matching genitals doesn’t … um … count in the same way? Or, indeed, sex where Persephone might be the one inside her lover?Come on, Hades. This is nonsense. Stop being weirdIn any case, while this wasn’t my favourite KR, I still got a lot out of Neon Gods. In particular, there’s a scene on the throne, which I won’t spoil, that is worth the price of admission alone. It is very much KR at the top of her game and an absolutely gorgeous moment. *chef’s kiss*.
to all the greek mythology girlies……..this was simply not good

I received an ARC from NetGalley in exchange for an honest review.Neon Gods is a contemporary romance retelling of Hades and Persephone. Olympus is a modern city that is ruled by the Thirteen and Zeus is the most powerful member of the Thirteen. When Demeter forces Persephone's betrothal to Zeus, Persephone escapes from the upper city of Olympus and crosses the River Styx to the lower city, where Hades rules.I finished this book with a thousand questions about the world building and character motivations.World BuildingIt sounds as if some members of the Thirteen are elected, whereas others are born into their role. The political structure of Olympus makes zero sense. It seems that Olympus is a city located in the United States of America. The reason I make this assumption is because Persephone mentioned she had plans to escape from Olympus to California. At the same time, Olympus seems to exist as a city outside of America. The world building is so vague. Where exactly does the City of Olympus fit in modern day America? What does the political structure look like? How does this city operate democratically when leaders are born into their roles? Is there a criminal justice system in place? Does the rule of law apply? It is hinted that Ares operates the military and police side of things, but once again, it is an assumption I am making.CharactersNeon Gods is marketed as a sunshine and grump romance, but I honestly did not see it that way. We are told that Hades is a grump, but he does not come across as a grump at all. Broody? Yes. Grumpy? No. Hades is a misunderstood hero who has built a fearsome reputation for himself. We are told Hades is intimidating and that he has a presence, but I didn't feel any of that. There is no danger or intrigue, which, in my opinion, kind of defeats the purpose of Hades. We are also told that Persephone has a sunshine disposition, which is a fake mask she wears in society. But we only get to see Persephone out in society for like 2 chapters, so we rarely see her as this "sunshiny" person. In my opinion, this version of Hades and Persephone just didn't fit their original mythological roles. Take out the one-dimensional mythology and you are left with a light mafiaeseque romance.The characters, especially the villains, have little to no depth. Why does Zeus hate Hades's parents so much? We never actually get an answer from Zeus, beyond his statement that Hades's father deserved to suffer. I was expecting a solid backstory to provide an explanation/motivation for Zeus's hatred toward Hades's parents. We are also told that Zeus is the most powerful member of the Thirteen. What exactly distinguishes him from the other members of the Thirteen? How did Zeus get powerful enough to get away with murder? How did Zeus get powerful enough to banish Hades from the rest of Olympus? Is there no rule of law in a city that is presumably located in America?Honestly, this book feels like a first draft. The world building is weak and has zero depth. The characters also leave a lot to be desired. The romance developed too quickly and the chemistry was lackluster. Neon Gods was very disappointing. I'm hoping the next book is better.
[reread jan. 2024] Yep I still fucking love this book and it's still my favorite in the series

“‘Yes, Sir’ is the proper response when we’re in this room.”I enjoyed this way more than I thought I would.I’m not all that familiar with the original Hades and Persephone, neither have I read a lot of retellings -A Court of Mist and Furybeing at the top - but this has definitely sparked my interest in wanting to read more.I do wish it was a bit more darker. Not that I’m complaining about how soft of a bastard Hades became for Persephone, but things seem to come pretty easy to them and I would’ve liked things to have been a bit more complex.What I mean to say is I wanted to see my characters suffer.But I enjoyed it a lot nonetheless. The audiobook was pretty great as well.And now I can’t wait to continue on with the series.
they really tried to distract us with a nice cover to hide the fact that there's a super questionable plot and no world-building whatsoever. fam, this aint my first rodeo 🤠Rating:1.75/5

And I thought Katee Robert just wrote good smut. I have so much to learn.I’ve been obsessed with Greek mythology since I was a teenager but as the years have passed I’ve kinda lost interest. But then I was inundated with Neon Gods all over TikTok and goodreads, so I figured it was time I reacquaint myself. I’ve always been an Athena kind of girl, (goes hand in hand with placing myself in Ravenclaw) but Hades was always my favorite male. Need I explain why? I think not.Ok, it’s a little ridiculous how many butterflies I feel in my belly when Hades catches her. Guess that obsession was much like riding a bike.I’m sure this is probably obvious to anyone that’s read ACOTAR series by Sarah J Maas. But it’s just occurred to me the reason I love Rhys so much is no doubt because he’s basically Hades.Wait ... wait a goddam minute! Did she just say playroom? Are we talking about the same kind of playroom? Because if we are this book just became even better than I could’ve possibly hoped.Oh Persephone is a delight as well. I think we’d be besties, we both speak fluent sarcasm.Chivalry is not dead, folks. Just ask Hades.Goddam! Katee has a knack for setting one overwhelmingly erotic stage. I might give up my soul for a night with this Hades.And she’s a brat which just makes this so much better.So, this wasn’t as bdsm forward as I had hoped, mostly voyuerism. But honestly, even though this one had spice that’s not why I loved it. I fucking adore Hades and Persephone. They were everything I wanted and more; adorable, sweet, funny and so loving. God, I’m gonna miss them!

book.title: Family Business
book.author: S.J. Rozan
book.description: The death of a powerful Chinatown crime boss thrusts private eye Lydia Chin and her partner Bill Smith into a world of double-dealing, subterfuge, murder, and—because this is New York City—real estate in this new mystery by Edgar Award-winning novelist S. J. Rozan.Choi has left the Tong headquarters building to his niece, who hires Lydia and her partner, Bill Smith, to accompany her to inspect it. The building is at the center of a tug-of-war between Chinatown preservation interests—including Lydia's brother Tim—and a real estate developer who's desperate to get his hands on it.When Lydia, Bill, and Choi's niece go to the building, they discover the Tong members are equally divided on the question of whether the niece should hold onto the building or sell it—and make them rich. Entering Choi's private living quarters, they find the murdered body of Choi's chief lieutenant.The battle for the building has begun. Can Lydia and Bill escape being caught in the crossfire?

reviews.text: 3.5 ☆InFamily Business,matters are kept close to home in a New York City that's untouched by Covid-19. The death of Big Brother Choi is huge news and flashes through the Chinatown community. Although Choi died from natural causes, the NYPD are put on alert because of the repercussions, in particular the new power vacuum in the tong. The Li Min Jin tong may style themselves as a community-minded nonprofit that sponsors activities like youth sports, but the Organized Crime division has them in their sight lines for their protection rackets and gambling dens."In Choi's day he was a terror. He whipped the tong into shape and got to work.... Choi was big on encroaching on other guys' territories which really isn't done. There was pushback, which meant violence."In the aftermath of Choi's death, the neighborhood is closely monitoring the fate of a proposed high-rise development led by Jackson Ting. His project needs to acquire the old Li Min Jin building in order for construction to proceed but Choi had refused to sell. Now that Choi is gone, whoever emerges as the new tong leader will determine the fate of this massive regentrification proposal.And then a spanner is thrown into the works as the interested parties learn who inherits the Li Min Jin building. The tong denizens expected their leader to bequeath it to the brotherhood. But no, Choi's heir is his niece, Melanie Wu, an attorney who can't afford the appearance of being linked with criminals. Melanie hires private investigator Lydia Chin to be her community advisor and bodyguard.At Choi's funeral, Lydia sees her brother Tim, who is on the board of directors with the Chinese Historical Society which opposes Ting's project.Until I was born he was the baby, so there's an understandable resentment there; also, he's a stodgy judgmental know-it-all wet blanket and has been since we were kids.It doesn't help that out of Lydia's four older brothers, Tim is the only one who still disapproves of her relationship with Bill Smith, her P.I. and her romantic partner. Ah, family -- can't live with them, can't live without them.Family Businessisn't the strongest installment in this series but it will offer hours of light diversion as Rozan had knitted a mystery with complicated family ties. On the positive side, I didn't anticipate what Rozan had ultimately dreamed up. But tong issues felt a bit retro as did Lydia's emotional regression because of sibling contention. The pacing was a bit slow as Rozan laid out the pertinent history for the key characters. The threat of violence was a constant but I can't say that it contributed a lot of tension to the storyline. After reading this entire series, I know that Rozan can do better and I suspect that the pandemic is to blame. This can be read as a standalone.

SJ Rozan’s latest Lydia Chin/Bill Smith title, Family Business, is a wonderful look at New York’s Chinatown, full of atmosphere and a strong sense of place. But it also addresses some complicated topics, starting on a small scale with Lydia’s own family dynamics, including her unstated “don’t ask/don’t tell” bargain with her very Chinese mother (who is maybe, just maybe, starting to warm up a little bit to the non-Chinese Smith) and her relationships with her four older brothers: two who think Smith is okay; one who thinks he’s great; and the fourth, who, in Lydia’s words, is “the only one who still dislikes [Smith] at all”. On a larger scale, Rozan also takes a look at the potential impacts, both good and bad, of gentrification on immigrant neighborhoods. And along the way, of course, there’s also a murder for Lydia and Bill to solve.The story opens with the apparently natural death of Big Brother Choi, the long-time head of one of Chinatown’s larger tongs. As Lydia’s high-school physics prof told her, “nature abhors a vacuum…[a]nd so does power”. So Choi’s death throws the tong into some disarray, not least because he has left the group’s HQ building to his niece, Mel Wu, rather than to the tong itself. And the building is already a source of controversy because it is the final “hold-out” preventing the 20-story “Phoenix Towers” housing development from being built.Lydia and Bill first get involved when Mel hires them to be discrete bodyguards during her visit to the building. Things really get messy, though, when the three find Choi’s lieutenant and heir apparent, Chang Yao-Zu, dead in the deceased Choi’s private quarters. And there’s no doubt this one is murder! Although the book starts off a little bit slow while setting all of this up, the story then takes off as Lydia and Bill try to keep Mel and her family safe, figure out who killed Chang, and avoid embarrassing Lydia’s stuffy brother, Tim – the one who still doesn’t like Bill.I have personally been a fan of Rozan’s Chin/Smith series for years, from its beginnings in the 1990s right through Rozan’s multi-year hiatus in the 2010s. And I am thrilled that she has started writing titles in the series again, with three books since 2019. Although Family Business is the fourteenth in the series, Rozan does a good job of providing enough background so both new and old readers can enjoy it. The story flows as smooth as silk, and is a great addition to the series. And I very much hope more titles are coming. Finally, I would like to thank Pegasus Books and Edelweiss for the advance review copy, which I received in exchange for my honest review.

I don't actually recall details from all the earlier volumes of this series (after all, it started almost thirty years ago), but I think that this is one of the best entries in this series. This is the fourteenth novel in S. J. Rozan's series about New York City private investigators Lydia Chin and Bill Smith, long-standing partners who have recently become lovers as well. The books alternate narration between the two protagonists.Family Businessis narrated by Lydia Chin, and hers is one of the families in the title.The elderly head of a New York Chinatown tong, the Li Min Jin, dies of a heart attack. It is expected that his "chief lieutenant," Chang Yao-Zu, will take over as leader of the tong. But there are other things that will be inherited. His personal fortune will be divided between two sisters, nieces of the late tong leader. Another inheritance goes to the elder sister solely; that is the building in which the tong was headquartered.That building is at the center of a controversy. A real estate developer needs that building as part of a large projected tower construction. People in the neighborhood oppose the construction: "twenty stories on the narrowest streets in New York," with the anticipated gentrification of the neighborhood. The tong has been divided about this. The man who died did not want to sell. Other tong members, not knowing that the leader's niece is inheriting the building, believe that their organization will inherit it, and they expect a huge windfall from the sale.The niece who is to inherit, Wu Mao-Li, known by her American name Mel (for Melanie) Wu, has been told by Chang Yao-Zu that her late uncle left a verbal message for her. She is to go to the tong headquarters to receive the message and see what is in the building. She asks Lydia and Bill to accompany her, to which they agree. When they go to the building, they find Chang's body; he has been stabbed to death.Now there is some question as to who should be in charge of the tong. The three main people likely to take over are Ironman Ma, a physically powerful and belligerent member of the tong, Loo Hu-Li, a long-standing member of the organization, and Tan Lu-Lien, one of the very few women to be a full member of the tong.The deceased former tong leader regarded the tong as part of his family, as he did his two nieces as well. He once had a wife and daughter, but the daughter died in childhood and his wife died soon afterward. But his is not the only family whose business is referred to in the title of the book.Lydia Chin's conservative real estate lawyer brother Tim, one of the two members of Lydia's family still opposed to her relationship with Bill, becomes increasingly involved in the story. Lydia's mother, also not accepting Bill, takes part in the story as well, as does Lydia's cousin Linus.The two nieces inheriting from the late tong leader are very close. The younger sister, Natalie Wu Harris, is married to a Caucasian man. They have two children. Her husband's family despises her because she is from an Asian family.The head of the real estate consortium seeking to buy the building is also Chinese-American. His family too will be important in the story.Chinatown itself is central to this book. Not only is the main concern a building there, much of the action is set there. I don't know how accurate Rozan's depiction of Chinatown in Manhattan is, but I find it very convincing. And the description of all the food consumed there throughout the book is amazing.I must add that there is one especially winning chapter in which Bill and Lydia meet with a woman who was originally from the Philippines. The discussion there includes other foods, particularly ube cookies, which also sound wonderful.Although Bill is half of the team, this is clearly Lydia Chin's book. Many of the other most important characters in the story - some of the bravest and brightest - are also women.I was not overly enthusiastic about the previous book in this series,The Art of Violence. I thought that much of it was extremely improbable.Family Businesshas its share of surprising developments, but I never found them to be beyond belief.Family Businesswas nominated for an Edgar Award for best mystery novel of the year. It is an excellent volume in a very fine series.

An excellent entry in an excellent series! Bill & Lydia are my new favorite detective duo . . . I popped in my AirPods to listen as I cleaned & became so absorbed that I just had to finish the story as I was running errands. Fortunately, there's a long backlist to make my way through. 😉

Excellent addition to the series. Lydia has the lead in this one, which is set in Manhattan's Chinatown.While the mystery was interesting, I really read this series to keep in touch with the characters. Of course we get a nice bit of Lydia's mother, with a bonus helping of Lydia's annoying lawyer brother, Ted. And the food! Oh my--do not read while hungry.

Fourteen books in and Rozan continues finding fresh material for her mismatched P.I. partners. Lydia Chin, a tiny dynamo with endless family in NYC's Chinese community, partners with Bill Smith, an extremely large prototypical P.I. The death of a tong elderly leader ties the future of the tong with the disposition of its headquarters. Along the way we learn many fascinating details about tongs, NY real estate and Chinese heritage while being treated to a twisty and engaging mystery. Highly recommended.
I really enjoyed this bookEaster Egg: Catch the books instead of boots error!

A strong 4+ rounded up, especially compared with the previous entry in the series.This story starts as a simple mystery surrounding the pressures on a woman who inherits a building in Chinatown that a variety of people would like to own. It seems straightforward, except that each conversation uncovers a new player, a new motivation, and a new secret. Just before the reveal, I admit to being a bit confused by the numerous players, red herrings, and subtle clues, but I wasn't frustrated. Of course, all is resolved, if a bit messily. Though there is a continuous undercurrent of criminal violence threatened, there are only two brief action scenes, and none of the primary characters are killed off.The side characters will be familiar to those who have read the entire series. Family and friends intertwine like a Virginia Reel, calling or popping up at just the right moment to provide the clue needed to push the story along.Other than the usual banter, Bill and Lydia don't really interact much. This is a story centered around Lydia's family and heritage; Bill is along just to look tough and hold the door.As always, the descriptions of places, clothing, smells and the food - oh the food! - are first rate.I'm hoping that there will be a more introspective and intimate story in the near future.

A very enjoyable read!! #14 in the Lydia Chin/Bill Smith Private Investigator series, SJ Rozan's award-nom'd FAMILY BUSINESS drops you right into Manhattan's Chinatown streets, complete with delicious food and scheming gangs, but with enough context that a reader who doesn't know anything about Chinatown doesn't feel lost. (That's a delicate balance; Rozan handles it well.) The "family business" of the title has two meanings here -- the "family business" of a gang that occupies a building that is the last holdout against a would-be developer and also the secretive interactions among members of several families, including Lydia's, with her four brothers and her mother, who is willing to step into the PI role when her daughter needs a hand. This book has Rozan's typical light, humorous repartee, between (first-person narrator) Lydia and Bill as well as Lydia and the reader, but it also digs deep into family loyalties and what they will cause some people to do. A nice twist at the end! This book works as a standalone and will likely intrigue you to go back to book #1 in the series, CHINA TRADE.

I didn't realize this was a series when I picked it up (there should be warning signs at the library - I like to start with #1!) but I enjoyed it nonetheless. I was looking for a fast paced mystery to get out of feeling bored with certain books and this definitely worked. I plan to read more of the novels in the series.

Another entry in a stellar series. Business and romantic partners Bill Smith and Lydia Chin are smart, funny, and bantering. This time out, things are happening in Chinatown. The death of an old tong leader has kicked off a contest to see what will happen to the building they operate out of.There are old and new alliances, financial and criminal, and old family secrets that all bear on the outcome. Lydia and Bill deftly navigate the treacherous world of the tongs and the world of costly New York real estate acquisition and development.As always, Lydia's interactions with her Chinese family are one of the most fun parts of the book. Understate and intelligent, and altogether a pleasurable read. This is one of the rare series where a romantic attachment between the main characters doesn't detract from the action.

This originally appeared atThe Irresponsible Reader.---“Big Brother Choi died."“Wow. What happened?”“Massive heart attack. Not to worry, there.”I hadn't really been worried. It wasn’t likely Big Brother Choi had been rubbed out by a rival tong leader and a Chinatown tong war was about to start. Those ham-handed days were largely gone.But Mary’s “there” implied something else, and I knew what it was, the reason Chris Chiang had called. A seismic shudder was about to move through the streets we'd grown up on.Our high school physics prof had told us that nature abhors a vacuum.So does power.WHAT'SFAMILY BUSINESSABOUT?The core of this novel is focused on what's going to happen to a real estate development in Chinatown. Which really doesn't sound that exciting—but Rozan's able to make you care. Also, this is not a run-of-the-mill real estate problem.There's a large-scale development headed towards Lydia's neighborhood, whether anyone wants it or not. The only thing stopping it is that the owner of one building in the middle of the projected development refuses to sell. That owner is—er, was—Big Brother Choi, the head of a tong, who uses that building as his tong's HQ.So, yeah, a criminal enterprise is all that stands between a (seemingly) legal and successful businessman and the destruction of a neighborhood/way of life. And then the head of the tong dies (of natural causes), and everything could change.Choi's niece—the executor of his will—comes to Lydia and Bill to provide some security for her as she tries to exert her position vis-à-vis the building. A lot is riding on this one building, and everyone has an opinion: there are two factions within the tong vying to determine the future of the building (and, with it, the tong); the developer; and the neighborhood's historical society--and it's all up to her to decide. People on all sides of the issue are trying to pressure her into making a decision they'd prefer about the building--and some of the pressure could be pretty intimidating.Especially when ranking members of the tong start dying—without natural causes like those that took out Choi. And then people start shooting at people tangentially connected to the niece and threatening them.Lydia and Bill now have to keep the niece alive and unscathed while trying to discover who's behind the shooting and the dead tong members.THE LYDIA AND BILL RELATIONSHIPThere are several reasons that Lydia and Bill's romantic relationship is one of my favorites in fiction, and I'm not going to try to enumerate them all. But probably my favorite part is that when they shifted from close friends/business associates to romantic partners the rest stayed pretty much the same. They deepened what was there, but didn't radically change it.Their flirting is a little more obvious—and Lydia's less likely to cut it off. But they still have each other's back, the mutual support and trust are still there. When they're on the job, they're on the job, not making goo-goo eyes at each other. Bill knows that Lydia is going to be reckless—and he doesn't try to stop her any more than he did before, but he sure worries about her. Would that everyone in fiction who made that transition did so as well.SO, WHAT DID I THINK ABOUTFAMILY BUSINESS?As I've mentioned before (likely too often), while I think most of the Bill-narrated novels are technically superior, the Lydia-narrated novels are consistently the more entertaining. So I went into this with high expectations—and they were pretty much met.I could go on about the flirting between Lydia and Bill, or talk about Lydia's wit and narration, or how nice it was to see a minor character from several books ago show up. But that's probably enough to indicate where I'm leaning with this post. There are a couple of points that I want to talk about a little, however.Ilovedthe way Lydia's mom inserted herself into the investigation. I've said enough about the slow-burn in getting the romance to advance, but getting Lydia's mother to stop actively disapproving of Lydia's career and now getting involved?? I dare you to go back 13 books and try to predict that.Like most people who read Detective Fiction, one of my great joys is being one or three steps ahead of the protagonist in putting the clues together. THere's something so satisfying about being more clever than they are—and in getting your guess endorsed by the protagonist. But what's better? Having the protagonist put all the pieces together in a way you didn't see coming—and instantly realizing there was no other way to interpret the evidence. That mix of "X was really clever there" and "ugh, I'm such a dunce for not seeing that" is somehow gratifying. For me, at least, the solutionStill, I'm not sure the people that Lydia initially revealed the solution to were the best to hear that. I'm even less sure of her timing on the revelation. But it did lead to some of the more exciting parts of the book—so as a reader, I liked that. I just wish that Lydia would be more judicious at times.This was my first novel of the year—it's a pretty good way to start 2022, something as entertaining as this was can make a guy optimistic about the next twelve months. That's probably a silly way to think about things, but I'm running with it.Family Businessis not only another strong entry in the series that will please fans, it like almost all those before) would be a good entry point for someone curious about the series. So really, there's no excuse to not pick it up. Go do so.

A well written, fast-paced mystery set in New York's Chinatown. This was my first book by this author featuring a pair of smart urban detectives, one of whom is Chinese. I enjoyed their interactions, the New York references, and learning about funeral practices, organized crime, and the threat of gentrification in Chinatown.It was entertaining, funny, and sweet at times.I will definitely go back and read the previous books in this series.
I love this series. The partners are quick and funny and the conflict of cultures is fascinating.

In this episode of the Lydia Chin/Bill Smith Chinatown, New York, series, an elderly friend of the Chin family contacts Lydia about helping Mel, a young woman who has inherited a building belonging to a local tong, a gang. Initially, Lydia and Bill are just going to accompany the client to the building to look around and receive a message that her uncle's lieutenant is supposed to pass along from the deceased.Unfortunately, the lieutenant is murdered just before the three arrive. Not only are there plenty of suspects from the tong, there's the issue of the message, and Mel still wants to receive it. She assumes it has something to do with the controversy surrounding the building, which some of the gangsters want to sell to a sketchy Chinese developer and which Chinatown residents and the rest of the gangsters want to save.As usual, Rozan's plot is tricky, with just the right level of hints. Her characters are all complicated and vibrant. Some are new. Others are old favorites, like the indispensable young cousin, a hacker who skirts the edge of the law to get information, and Lydia's childhood friend Mary, a police detective who is both furious and reluctantly grateful whenever Lydia gets involved in a dangerous case. This particular book also has a great role for Lydia's ornery mother, who doesn't like Bill, a Caucasian, but has apparently reached an uneasy truce about him.I love S.J. Rozan's mysteries. She may be tired of writing them, as suggested by a long hiatus, but she doesn't take it out on the reader the way some tired mystery writers seem to. And the ethnic food descriptions alone are worth the price of admission. Don't read this on empty stomach.

“Family Business,” by S.J. Rozan, Pegasus Crime, 352 pages, Dec. 7, 2021.Big Brother Choi Meng, a powerful Chinatown crime boss, dies. Choi has left the Tong headquarters building to his niece, Wu Mao-Li, who hires private investigator Lydia Chin and her partner, Bill Smith, to accompany her to inspect it.The building is at the center of a tug-of-war between Chinatown preservation interests—including Lydia's brother Tim—and Jackson Ting, a real estate developer who's desperate to get his hands on it for a large housing project. Wu Mao-Li, who goes by the name of Mel, is a real estate attorney. She is also executor of the estate.When Lydia, Bill, and Mel go to the building, they discover the Tong members are equally divided on the question of whether the niece should hold onto the building or sell it and make them rich. Entering Choi's private living quarters, they find the murdered body of Choi's chief lieutenant, Chang Yao-Zu.“Family Business” is intricately plotted with surprising twists. The characters are outstanding. The details of life in Chinatown, of well-established families and new immigrants, is fascinating. Lydia’s relationship with her mother, brothers and cousin seems like a real family. There is also a wonderful character who is an adopted grandfather. This is the 14th in the series, but you don’t have to read the others to enjoy it.In accordance with FTC guidelines, the advance reader's edition of this book was provided by the publisher via Edelweiss in exchange for a review.

I have missed Lydia Chin, Bill Smith and Manhattan's Chinatown. One of the things I like about this series is the infusion of Chinese culture--the interesting customs and characters.I did not read the previous book in the series, since the writer has intentionally made the books with Bill as protagonist more violent than the alternate books that have Lydia as protagonist. Apparently there was a change in their relationship in that story, so I may read it for that reason.The book prior to that was written after a long break in the series, and it was set in the south where Bill and Lydia went at the behest of her mother to help a paper son who was considered by her father as some kind of relation. I did not finish that book because the story was uninteresting and the pace was too slow.In Family Business, Bill and Lydia are back investigating a case in Chinatown for a Chinese-American client. Everything about this book is satisfying and familiar. The characters, setting, and dialog are again examples of Rozan at her best. Her writing is concise and has that trademark humor that the series is known for.I learned some fascinating facts about Chinese funeral and burial customs. Rozan is not Chinese and she doesn't credit a source for this info, but I want to think that it is authentic.I have never read why the author decided to write this series, and I would love to know. Whatever the reason, I am just glad that this series exists because it is one of my favorites.

Big Brother Choi, leader of a prominent Tong in Chinatown, dies suddenly, leaving the building housing the Tong to his niece. The new owner, Mel, hires Lydia Chin and Bill Smith to accompany her on her first inspection of the building, where they find the murdered body of the second-in-command. The Tong is divided over what should happen to the building, which many expected Choi to leave to the Tong. Complicating matters is Jackson Ting, a prominent NYC developer who wants the building to make possible a huge development that will significantly alter Chinatown. Mel refuses to say what she plans to do with the building—keep it, sell it, rent it to the Tong, or something else. Everyone wants an answer, and each side has ways to persuade her.Complicating matters is the role of Lydia's brother Tim, who still doesn't like Bill, and the role of their mother. The family banter is, as always, a delight, and the insights into traditional Chinese culture fascinating. There are plenty of twists and surprises, but it all makes perfect sense, and all the pieces fit together snugly and clearly.Highly recommended.

From Lydia Chin’s mother’s point of view, the Chin family is not doing badly. She has grandchildren. Lydia’s brothers – Andrew, a freelance photographer; Ted, an organic chemistry professor; Elliot, an ER doctor – are all well-established. Even the youngest brother Ted, has been made partner at his law firm. Now he just needs to find a nice Chinese girl and settle down. Lydia herself is still a disappointment, in a not-very-suitable job as a private investigator and far too involved with Bill Smith, her business partner.The Li Min Jin Tong is not doing quite so well – their leader Big Brother Choi having just died from a heart attack. A struggle for leadership of the tong is inevitable and the best qualified, Tan Lu-Lien, is not even in the running because she is a woman. The conflict coalesces around the building that is the home of the tong and has been bequeathed to Bi Brother Choi’s niece, Mel Wu, who is Lydia and Bill’s client.The writing is crisp and evocative, completely drawing the reader into the Chinatown setting.

Although there is a lot of violence, gunmen on motorcycles, heavy handed petty criminals, and unnamed men with dark impassive faces, the title really sums up the motivation behind most of the action.A long respected leader in Chinatown has died, leaving behind a message for a favorite niece, as well as bequeathing to her a building in Chinatown. The building is at the center of a real estate dispute - should it be sold to developers or kept in its present use for its many residents?Lydia and Bill are hired to provide security for the new owner, but not surprisingly get caught up in the conflicting aims of the other characters in the story.And in the end, family relationships, and family loyalties are at the heart of it all.

It has been years since I read a Bill and Lydia book and I missed them.This is a very clever plot and non stop action.I am not sure how ethical it is to have four clients paying for the same investigation, but the $1 from Tim can't be considered one of them.There is a real estate deal in the works and Chinatown is split as to it helping the community.The side that wants the new development will stop at nothing, including intimidation and kidnapping to get their way.I found the tong information to be very interesting, as are the various Chinese traditions concerning death and burial.All in all a very interesting and quick read.I borrowed a copy from the public library.

Family Business by S.J. Rozan is part of the author's Lydia Chin/Bill Smith detective series. This book was published in 2021.The story starts with the last will and testament of a local gangster. This is what brings the two private investigators into the crime world of their neighborhood Chinatown.At the heart of the fight are the fractions who want to protect the neighborhood versus the developer, and who has inherited the rights to the building.Rozan is author of eighteen crime novels and has won multiple awards for her fiction.If you enjoy the detective side of murder mysteries set in a big city venue I highly recommend this read.

This was a very fun read because it felt like the reader was really visiting Chinatown. The author did her research and really knows the local hangouts in Chinatown. Some knit picky things though that sometimes ruined the effect, such as sometimes using mandarin pronunciations and spelling, the cantonese family in the story would always use the cantonese expressions.There was little character development but didn't expect there to be. The plot was fast paced and the twists made it a lot of fun.

Lydia Chin and Bill Smith are PI's hired by a woman who inherits a NY Chinatown building that houses a powerful Chinese tong. With factions of the tong struggling to gain power since the death of their leader, the client's uncle, is followed by the murder of his successor. Add to this rumors of hidden treasures in the building and possible embezzlement by the tong's female CFO, Lydia and Bill have their hands full. Lots of action, Asian tradition and family dysfunction makes for an interesting read.

I am so glad S.J. Rozan has continued this series featuring Lydia Chin and Bill Smith. This time they are hired to help the niece of the head of the tong go and check out her inheritance of the tongs building. Upon arrival they find the First Lieutenant of the tong dead. What was the message he had for the niece? What does someone not want her to understand. While is deciding to keep or sell the building blackmail and kidnappings are used to make her do the opposite of what she wants.

Five stars given. Credit given, where credit is due. The writing is very good and the plot development first rate. The characters, well represented. I might be a bit biased, since when younger, working/visiting NYC also China Town, so many of the locations bring fond memories. Ten Ren's tea shop comes instantly to mind and I still order the 403 Green Ginseng Oolong. However, the five stars are well deserved, the entire series is simply great.

Family Business is the latest mystery novel in Rozan's long-running series featuring New York private eye Lydia Chin and her fellow P.I. boyfriend Bill Smith. Here, Lydia and Bill get pulled in to solve the mystery of a murdered Tong leader found dead in a Chinatown building at the center of a real estate development battle. There’s plenty of action, some light humor, and enough complexity to keep your attention right to the end.

Spoiler - Can someone who’s read the book please tell me who killed Chang Yao zu? I seem to have completely missed this part. Or did they even say?Overall the book was ok. It was interesting enough to make me want to know how it ends, but it was not super engaging up until the last 30-50 pages or so. The story seemed slow to develop and conversations seemed to repeat themselves (within the same conversation) while also not moving the plot forward?

Lydia and Bill are hired to help the niece of a dead tong leader when her uncle leaves her a building that houses the tong. Needless to say, these things are never easy and nothing goes as planned. Filled with some drama, a great mystery, and SJ's wonderful sense of humor, this is a must-read for fans of the Lydia and Bill books.

The return of Lydia Chin and Bill Smith is always good news and because this murder takes place in NYC Chinatown there is lots for us to learn about customs in addition to the quirky members of Lydia's family. I like the deepening of the Chin/Smith relationship.don't make us wait so long for the next chapterl

book.title: Be Careful, It's My Heart
book.author: Kait Nolan
book.description: The best things happen while you're acting...The historic Madrigal theater, in the heart of downtown Wishful, is about to close its doors forever. A last ditch fund-raising effort, a production of White Christmas, is probably the only thing that could bring Tyler Edison out of retirement. She fell in love on that stage, but when Brody Jensen abandoned her, she lost the heart to sing and dance for the crowd. Maybe it's time to take that back...It seems like pure chance when Brody's job, which has taken him all over the world, brings him back to his little hometown to oversee his boss's latest secret project. Brody's looking for closure, planning to sell the house his parents left him and finally put his past, and his memories of Tyler, behind him. What better way than to be a part of this last show? Even though his leading lady is surely long gone...Put on your dancing shoes, auditions start at six o'clock.

reviews.text: I knew that I would love this book because the quaint southern town of Wishful is putting on one last musical in the Madrigal Theater and it just happens to be based on my all time favorite movie, White Christmas. When Tyler and her best friend Piper hear the news that the Madrigal Theater will have to close due to lack of operating funds, the two friends dust off their dancing shoes and are thrilled to be named the two female leads in the play. But things aren't so rosy when Tyler discovers she has to play opposite her old boyfriend Brody who skipped town without a word 8 years ago and broke her heart. The musical storyline is absolutely delightful and, as with all things in Wishful, the entire town is determined to save both the theater and the romance between Tyler and Brody. Well-meaning meddling characters and a heartwarming second chance romance combined with musical theater. What could be better than that? I listened to the audiobook and thoroughly enjoyed the entertaining narration performance of Amy McFadden

Tyler Edison runs the hardware store like three generations of her family before her. She hasn’t danced or sung publicly since her favourite partner left town, but they both put their names down for a production intended to raise money to restore the local theatre.Brody left town eight years ago and is back working on a secret project for the town’s fairygodfather, Peyton. For some reason, he never expected her to be exactly where he left her.[really? How well did he know her? They dated for four years…]Oh no… I’ve never seen White Christmas. This might not work.Wow, she seems really unconcerned when her friend Tucker breaks his leg. She’s more distressed that Brody will have to step up from understudy.*eyes narrow* I’m not sure I even like Tyler.Oddest thing… Brody goes to the hardware store and overhears Tyler saying ‘Hey baby. Did you and Grandpa have a good day today?’ to what he thinks is a child, Ollie. Later when asked, she says he’s seven. Brody does the math and comes up with baby and runs off down the secret baby trope path.Now,so did I.So later when it turns out Ollie is a dog, I feel as betrayed and stupid as Brody did. Second hand embarrassment for the win. And that makes me kind of annoyed. I was ready to quit but I kept reading.I finished but… there’s a lot of drama club nonsense here.First off, Brody asks her dad if he can marry her. (what century IS this?) I don’t know what he said, it isn’t mentioned again. I guess… no? Does Tyler even know about this? Why did Dad say no? Brody was 21, owned a house in town, they’d dated for four years, they have similar interests, and he was training to be an architect or whatever. I just don’t remember this being dealt with. If it was the damn hardware store… *grits teeth*Next he leaves town without speaking to her but writes a letter enclosing a ticket to Paris and says he’ll meet her at the top of the Eiffel Tower. He waits all day. She doesn’t come.So they haven’t watched ‘An Affair to remember’? How many ways can this go wrong?Besides the fact that you CAN’T stay at the top for very long (they shuffle people out quick smart) what if she didn’t have a passport? Paris? It’s romantic but bloody impractical.The snowplow took out the mail box or something random and she never got the letter.I went and skimmed to check… he never asked HER.How dare he audition for the show as if nothing had happened, as if he hadn’t disappeared with a word, without a trace, without a freaking goodbye. (Kindle Locations 411-413).He interpreted silence and no Paris as No.But what the hell did you say to a man when the last time you saw him, you asked permission to marry his daughter? The same daughter who didn’t want to marry you and was standing across the counter? (Kindle Locations 623-624).And neither ever contacted the other to ask if they were okay, or if they wanted to be with them, or anything at all.FOR EIGHT YEARS.Communication is a good thing. He still owned his parent’s house and she could have messaged him via whoever was managing that for him even if he was travelling.She fell apart for two years and her Dad never told her about the proposal? *frowns*The first time they kiss again is onstage in their roles. It called for a peck on the cheek and he goes all in. Dude… not cool.Another story point not dealt with: how did his parents die? Why did some say it was his fault? I don’t remember reading that.The characters from book 1: Norah is here but Cam only rates an appearance in one scene.Tucker faked the broken leg. For weeks. And nobody noticed. Again… drama club stuff.Then there is an issue with the theatre and Tyler overhears him talking to his boss, misinterprets what he says, and breaks up with him over it.This is the FOURTH time in two books that this story point has been done.I would be okay with all of this manufactured drama if I liked the characters… but I kind of don’t.And bugger rebuilding the theatre. This town needs to spend some cash on communication classes.2 stars

This is Book 3 in the Wishful series. These should be read//listened to in order as storylines build on events/characters/situations from previous books. While titles are sold individually, the series has been collected into box sets of 3 books each. Be Careful, It’s My Heart is part of Volume 1 which is available in ebook & audio formats.This one didn’t work for me as well as the other stories in the series and I’m pretty sure it’s because I have never seen White Christmas - in either its play or movie form. Since the bulk of the story revolves around the production of the play for the town of Wishful and there were a lot of references to the storyline of White Christmas, I wasn’t able to connect with it as fully as I think I would have.That said, it’s still a cute second-chance romance. A big misunderstanding sent Brody & Tyler on separate paths years ago and a lack of communication threatens to keep them from working things out this time. Queue the positively-meddling folks of Wishful and their joined forces to save an aging theatre while simultaneously keeping Brody & Tyler from making the same mistakes.Amy McFadden continues giving an expanding townsfolk their own unique voices, while keeping old favorites consistent from previous books. I don’t know if McFadden color codes these characters or what - it’s definitely an impressive rainbow of voices she has in her repertoire.

If you are in the mood for a lighthearted story, then I suggest “Be Careful, It’s My Heart.”This was a fun, quick read – but not so quick that it felt rushed. The characters were charming and the plot fun. Kait Nolan has a wonderful skill of writing tension. This kept me turning pages.The town of Wishful, Mississippi is a great setting. I certainly hope Ms Nolan continues this series. My only critique is, I hoped for more atmosphere and mood from the small town setting – more physical description. I think I want this because I know I will love the scene Kait will paint.If you’ve read any of the short novels from the Harlequin Kiss line of books, you will LOVE this title.I’m eager to read more.

Watching light, romantic Christmas movies is one of the best stress-busters of the holiday season. Want to curl up with one in book form? Join the cast of Kait Nolan's BE CAREFUL, IT'S MY HEART as they prepare for a stage production of our favorite Christmas classic, White Christmas. It's the kind of quick, engrossing read that will take you to another place as you hide out from annoying relatives on Thanksgiving, or if you need to take a break from your hectic holiday preparations in the charming town of Wishful, Mississippi.

That's two books in a row where some of the conflict comes from one person (in this case the heroine) overhearing part of a conversation and jumping to conclusions. The pain caused is completely self-inflicted and avoidable, and it made me not like the characters as much. Worse than that, the initial conflict comes from another miscommunication caused by the hero, which already made it hard to like him. Then she does her jump to conclusions thing, and i'm finding it hard to like her either.
This is the first book of this author I have read and not the last. I would like to know the stories of the rest of her heroes.

Oh my goodness! I absolutely loved Tyler and Brody! I’m so happy they got a second chance! Even though they had their ups and downs in the end it all worked out for the best! Can’t wait to go back to this small town and see how everyone is doing! 🥰🎶💛
Adorable read.Second chance romance because of a miscommunication.Tyler and Brodys book.Looking forward to pipers book.I do really like these small town romances.
My jaw dropped at the final scene. I loved it.

This is a fun second-chance romance (one of my favorite Romance tropes) with theatre as a backdrop, which worked perfectly for me. I loved the setting and enjoyed the characters a lot. The only part I disliked was the multiple cases of not talking to each other that complicated everything, but since that was the whole pivot of the "second-chance" part of the story, you can't really take that part out. At least the pair's friends all tried to get them to actually talk to each other.
🎁 FREE on Amazon & on iBooks today (3/2/2020)! 🎁

Ms Nolan takes us back to Wishful , a Southern town filled with fun and interesting characters.This one gives us Tyler Edison and Brody Jensen. These two were sweethearts until the day, eight years ago, he left her behind to pursue a life away from the town, or so she thought.Brody is back in town for his work as a general contractor. They both tryout for the play White Christmas .and thus have to face each other. At one time they were the couple everyone one loved to watch dance and act in the local theatre. Now this theatre is about to be closed down and the last “Hail Mary” is a fundraising .All that said, it’s a story about second chances. Also , it’s a story about how misunderstandings and assumptions are that can cause more harm than good.Will these two clear the air, and find that they do have a second chance? Or , will be stubborn and not take a chance?The story is well developed and has likable characters. You will laugh at times, and want to meet entire town .I was gifted this audiobook. The opinions expressed are solely my own.

I enjoyed reading this book a lot. It is the first in the series I've read, now I'm going back to read the first one. I loved the small town and the characters. The author did a great job of setting them in my mind with her words. It is a great story that is just light enough to be an easy fun read. I recommend this book and I can't wait to read the others in the series.

The trouble with basing a story around some other real-world media is that sometimes, no matter how famous or popular that iconic thing is, there will be a reader who hasn't ever seen it.Just like I have never seen the movieWhite Christmas, so when the two main characters are cast in a last-ditch, save-the-theater musical version of the movie (very pointedly NOT the original musical) I was adrift at the story references.On the upside, I'm a former musical theater geek myself, so it won some points for that. I was never in a leading role and I certainly never got to have a musical-fueled romance, but I'm absolutely down with that as the basis for a story, color me swooning.On the downside, this is a second-chance romance based on a breakup that was A STUPID COLOSSAL MISUNDERSTANDING THAT COULD HAVE EASILY BEEN SOLVED IF THE CHARACTERS HAD ACTUALLY TALKED TO EACH OTHER.They did at least admit that, when it came out that their entire eight-year interlude of misery could have been prevented, but my feelings on misunderstandings as conflict are dim at best.So this was a mixed bag for me, because I loved some parts while hating others. Definitely continuing on with the series, though.

To be honest, I must have missed something in this book. I don’t get the title at all. But—it doesn’t matter! I still loved it. This book is short and sweet. Somehow Kait Nolan has stuffed a full length romance into this little novella, leaving this reader thoroughly satisfied yet still wanting more. If you love a good classic romance, this is the winter read for you. The White Christmas backdrop imbues this book with a nostalgic, feel good vibe that resonates through every moment of the story. The chemistry between Tyler and Brody is either what you want or a needed reminder of what you have. And Nolan seasons this Happily Ever After with a solid dose of reality—this is not an all or nothing kind of ride. Nolan keeps it real, understanding that love often requires some kind of compromise.I read this book in one sitting and I can’t wait for the next installment. I hope Nolan gives Tyler and Brody’s best buds their moment in the moonlight.(As published on ChickLitPlus.com; I received a copy of Be Careful, It’s My Heart by Kait Nolan in exchange for an honest review.)

I'm learning to love the little town of Wishful and all the people that live there, and in Be Careful, It's My Heart we get to watch the Happily Ever After of another pair of Wishful's residents. It's a short read but I didn't realize it was until I finished the story, looked at the clock, and realized it hadn't taken nearly as long as it felt. Not in a bad way; I was just so invested in the story. It doesn't rush and it doesn't drag - the pacing is perfect. The characters are so real that you want to take them and shake them when they're being especially dense because it feels like you want them to get their happily-ever-after more than they do. This sweet, sexy romance was thoroughly enjoyable and I highly recommend it.I received a complimentary copy from the author.

Another visit to Wishful. This time to see Tyler and Brody get back together after miscommunications and misunderstandings have kept them apart.
